title: Services, Units, Boot State, and Logs · Operations Case: Service Unit Model
summary: Diagnose a service that is enabled but unavailable by separating desired boot state, current process state, dependencies, configuration, and logs. Planned artifact: Add and verify the linux service unit model increment in a service lifecycle and journal evidence packet. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Relate service managers, units, dependencies, targets, process supervision, and application readiness.
Define backup scope, consistency, retention, integrity, isolation, and restore testing as separate controls.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.shell:
# linux-basics cumulative operations evidence notebook
# Commands run only in the deterministic stateful terminal simulator.
# Record hypothesis, command, expected signal, observation, conclusion, and recovery.

steps.title: Predict Service Unit Model
steps.instruction:
theory case — lab-worker.service is enabled and active, but readiness failed after configuration revision 4. Query effective unit settings and journal events from this boot before any restart or rollback.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Service Unit Model contract for the supplied data.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for relate service managers, units, dependencies, targets, process supervision, and application readiness.

content.text: lab-worker.service is enabled and active, but readiness failed after configuration revision 4. Query effective unit settings and journal events from this boot before any restart or rollback.

content.title: Commit before feedback
content.text: Choose from the stated data before opening the worked model. Record which Service Unit Model condition decided your prediction.

content.label: Authority to consult after committing
content.value: GNU Coreutils Manual — Controls file, text, identity, process, filesystem, and common utility behavior.

hints: Name the Service Unit Model precondition or invariant that the theory case must preserve.

Use the documented misconception as a counterexample target: An enabled service is necessarily running and healthy now.

steps.title: Bound Service Unit Model
steps.instruction:

After tracing the worked code, choose the Service Unit Model application whose preconditions and limits still match the supplied case.

content.text: Relate service managers, units, dependencies, targets, process supervision, and application readiness. Use this as an observable contract: name its preconditions, trace the state or value change, and state what evidence would disprove the result.

content.title: Reasoning move
content.text: Capture identity, path, process, file, and service state; predict parsing and scope; run one safe simulated observation; interpret exit status; then change one bounded layer and verify rollback.

content.title: Worked case pattern
content.text: lab-worker.service is enabled and active, but readiness failed after configuration revision 4. Query effective unit settings and journal events from this boot before any restart or rollback. A defensible solution applies Service Unit Model, preserves the governing invariant or value contract, and connects the observed result to a service lifecycle and journal evidence packet.

content.code:
systemctl show lab-worker.service -p ActiveState -p SubState -p Result
journalctl -u lab-worker.service -b --since '10 minutes ago'
# Restart only after evidence identifies a reversible cause.
content.caption: Runnable safe terminal evidence example; predict its result before running, then change one boundary input.

content.title: Non-worked case
content.text: An enabled service is necessarily running and healthy now. This fails the Service Unit Model contract because it omits a precondition, invariant, ownership rule, or counterexample needed by this exact case.

steps.title: Build Service Unit Model evidence
steps.instruction: Append a safe Linux operations investigation headed "# Evidence: linux-service-unit-model-qx0xau" with a concrete hypothesis, one bounded simulator command, and an observation that changes the next decision.
steps.why: Building a runnable or mechanically reviewable artifact turns Service Unit Model into observable performance evidence.

options.text: The record names the assumption, applies Service Unit Model, and reports a changed-case check tied to the stakeholder decision.

steps.title: Defend the Service Unit Model model
steps.instruction: Write the theory evidence note for Service Unit Model: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — lab-worker.service is enabled and active, but readiness failed after configuration revision 5. Query effective unit settings and journal events from this boot before any restart or rollback.
steps.why: Retrieval and explanation expose gaps that recognition questions can hide.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Relate service managers, units, dependencies, targets, process supervision, and application readiness.
